---
title: "First Data Exploration"
format: html
editor: visual
---

# Data Exploration

The first data access and exploration for understanding and investigation before downstream performance. From the original source folder, the directory has been copied to my personal directory.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1. Setting Up

### 1.1 Packages

Installing the packages needed

```{r, message = FALSE}
library(ggplot2)# For visual plotting
library(dplyr) # For dataframe manipulation
```

### 1.2 Working Directory

Double checking the working directory location

```{r}
setwd("~/Twins_Project/notebooks/data_exploration")
```

### 1.3 Save path

Defining an output path where I will save the visualisations generated to.

```{r}
output_path <- "~/Twins_Project/visualisations"
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2. 'dataset.RData' Exploration

This file contains the large matrix of immunophenotypes and inflammatory protein variables. Relevant covariates are included as well. Information of this dataframe is also provided, describing the immunophenotypes. The data is precleaned.

### 2.1 Loading the data

```{r}
# Loading/Restoring the dataset from personal directory to current Global Environment
load("~/Twins_Project/data/raw_olink_ip/data/dataset.RData")
```

After loading, dataset.RData contains two objects; dataset and info.

### 2.2 Structure Checks

Exploring how the data is organised and what data types it features.

```{r}
head(dataset) # Quick view
dim(dataset) # Exploring dimensions
```

From the dimensions, there are 485 rows, signifying that there are 485 unique individuals. The first 23 columns contain metadata and covariates. Columns 24 to 97 contain the proteomic data of the inflammatory proteins from the Olink panel. Column 98 is iid which is the individual ID. Then the rest of the columns (99 to 42393) contain the immunophenotypes.

### 2.3 Exploring the Metadata

Understanding the population.

#### 2.3.1 Sex and Zygosity

```{r}
print(table(dataset$SEX)) # Sex
print(table(dataset$Z)) # Zygote
```

All individuals are females and there are 333 dizygotic/fraternal and 152 monozygotic/identical of twins.

To understand whether individuals were tested with their pairs or alone, analysis of FID (family ID) is required.

```{r}
#Finding non repeated FID
dataset %>%
  group_by(fid) %>%
  filter(n() ==1) %>%
  pull (fid)
```

11 individuals were tested without their pair. This could be due to factors like consent, schedule...

#### 2.3.2 Age Statistics

```{r}
cat("Mean Age: ", mean(dataset$Age), "\n") #Mean Age
cat("SD Age:   ", sd(dataset$Age), "\n") #Standard deviation
cat("Youngest: ", min(dataset$Age), "\n") #Youngest
cat("Oldest:   ", max(dataset$Age), "\n") #Oldest
cat("Median:   ", median(dataset$Age), "\n") #Median
```

note : Perhaps can do a age plot with mz/dz distribution population pyramid plot..

##### 2.3.2.1 Graphing Age distribution

```{r}
dir.create(output_path, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
#setting the age group distribution 
dataset_clean <- dataset %>%
  mutate(Age_Bin = cut(Age, breaks = seq(40, 80, by = 5),labels = c("40-44", "45-49", "50-54", "55-59", "60-64", "65-69", "70-74", "75-79"), right = FALSE))

#using ggplot to create a stacked bar chart 
age_zyg_plot<- ggplot(dataset_clean, aes(x = Age_Bin, fill = Z)) +
                  geom_bar(position = "stack", color = "white", size = 0.2) +
                  #adding in the numbers in the bars for distribution 
                  stat_count(geom = "text", aes(label = after_stat(count)), 
                             position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), size = 3, color = "black") +
                  labs(
                    title = "Age and Zygosity Distribution (Raw)",
                    x = "Age Group ",
                    y = "Number of Individuals",
                    fill = "Zygosity"
                  ) +
                  scale_fill_manual(values = c("DZ" =  "#D1C4E9", "MZ" = "#745A92")) +
                  theme_minimal() +
                  theme(
                    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
                    panel.grid = element_blank(), #remove grid lines
                    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1)
                  )

#Saving plot
ggsave(
  plot = age_zyg_plot,
  filename = "age_zyg_plot.png", 
  path = output_path,
  width = 10,     
  height = 5
)
```

2.3.2.2 Remove unpaired twins.

```{r}
#For downstream analysis, only want complete pair so we need to remove the singletons
dataset_paired<- dataset %>%
  group_by(fid) %>%
  filter(n() == 2) %>%  
  ungroup()
```

Now find the distribution of twins in the paired set

```{r}
print(table(dataset_paired$Z)) # Zygote
```

```{r}
cat("Mean Age: ", mean(dataset_paired$Age), "\n") #Mean Age
cat("SD Age:   ", sd(dataset_paired$Age), "\n")
cat("Youngest: ", min(dataset_paired$Age), "\n") #Youngest
cat("Oldest:   ", max(dataset_paired$Age), "\n") #Oldest
cat("Median:   ", median(dataset_paired$Age), "\n") #Median
```

Recreate the plot with only paired twins

```{r}
#setting the age group distribution 
dataset_pairs <- dataset_paired %>%
  mutate(Age_Bin = cut(Age, breaks = seq(40, 80, by = 5),labels = c("40-44", "45-49", "50-54", "55-59", "60-64", "65-69", "70-74", "75-79"), right = FALSE))

#using ggplot to create a stacked bar chart 
age_zyg_plot<- ggplot(dataset_pairs, aes(x = Age_Bin, fill = Z)) +
                  geom_bar(position = "stack", color = "white", size = 0.2) +
                  #adding in the numbers in the bars for distribution 
                  stat_count(geom = "text", aes(label = after_stat(count)), 
                             position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), size = 3, color = "black") +
                  labs(
                    title = "Age and Zygosity Distribution (Incomplete Pairs Removed)",
                    x = "Age Group ",
                    y = "Number of Individuals",
                    fill = "Zygosity"
                  ) +
                  scale_fill_manual(values = c("DZ" =  "#D1C4E9", "MZ" = "#745A92")) +
                  theme_minimal() +
                  theme(
                    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
                    panel.grid = element_blank(), #remove grid lines
                    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1)
                  )

#Saving plot
ggsave(
  filename = "rm_age_zyg_plot.png", 
  path = output_path,
  width = 10,     
  height = 5
)
```

#### 2.3.3 QC Warning

In downstream analysis, we may have to omit those that did not pass the Olink QC (not sure if this is applies to bioinformatics pipeline). But if we remove those individuals, we are left with singleton twins.

```{r}
#Finding how many passed and those who got warnings
print(table(dataset$QC)) 

dataset %>%
  group_by(`QC Warning`) %>%
  filter(`QC Warning` == "Warning") %>%
  pull (id)
```

We need to remove those 7 and their twin so 14 individuals.

```{r}
#Final filtering after QC warning 
dataset_final<- dataset_paired %>%
  filter(`QC Warning` == "Pass") %>%
  group_by(fid) %>%
  filter(n() == 2) %>% 
  ungroup()
```

Graphing the qc filtered age distribution

```{r}

#setting the age group distribution 
dataset_clean <- dataset_final %>%
  mutate(Age_Bin = cut(Age, breaks = seq(40, 80, by = 5),labels = c("40-44", "45-49", "50-54", "55-59", "60-64", "65-69", "70-74", "75-79"), right = FALSE))

#using ggplot to create a stacked bar chart 
age_zyg_plot<- ggplot(dataset_clean, aes(x = Age_Bin, fill = Z)) +
                  geom_bar(position = "stack", color = "white", size = 0.2) +
                  #adding in the numbers in the bars for distribution 
                  stat_count(geom = "text", aes(label = after_stat(count)), 
                             position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), size = 3, color = "black") +
                  labs(
                    title = "Age and Zygosity Distribution (Final - After QC)",
                    x = "Age Group ",
                    y = "Number of Individuals",
                    fill = "Zygosity"
                  ) +
                  scale_fill_manual(values = c("DZ" = "#D1C4E9", "MZ" = "#745A92")) +
                  theme_minimal() +
                  theme(
                    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
                    panel.grid = element_blank(), #remove grid lines
                    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1)
                  )

#Saving plot
ggsave(
  filename = "final_age_zyg_plot.png", 
  path = output_path,
  width = 10,     
  height = 5
)
```

```{r}
print(table(dataset_final$Z)) # Zygote
cat("Mean Age: ", mean(dataset_final$Age), "\n") #Mean Age
cat("SD Age:   ", sd(dataset_final$Age), "\n")
cat("Youngest: ", min(dataset_final$Age), "\n") #Youngest
cat("Oldest:   ", max(dataset_final$Age), "\n") #Oldest
cat("Median:   ", median(dataset_final$Age), "\n") #Median
```

Checking the age distribution of MZ

```{r}
print("MZ")
cat("Mean Age: ", mean(dataset_final$Age[dataset_final$Z == "MZ"]), "\n") #Mean Age
cat("SD Age:   ", sd(dataset_final$Age[dataset_final$Z == "MZ"]), "\n")
cat("Youngest: ", min(dataset_final$Age[dataset_final$Z == "MZ"]), "\n") #Youngest
cat("Oldest:   ", max(dataset_final$Age[dataset_final$Z == "MZ"]), "\n") #Oldest
cat("Median:   ", median(dataset_final$Age[dataset_final$Z == "MZ"]), "\n") #MediaN

```

Checking the age distribution of DZ

```{r}
print("DZ")
cat("Mean Age: ", mean(dataset_final$Age[dataset_final$Z == "DZ"]), "\n") #Mean Age
cat("SD Age:   ", sd(dataset_final$Age[dataset_final$Z == "DZ"]), "\n")
cat("Youngest: ", min(dataset_final$Age[dataset_final$Z == "DZ"]), "\n") #Youngest
cat("Oldest:   ", max(dataset_final$Age[dataset_final$Z == "DZ"]), "\n") #Oldest
cat("Median:   ", median(dataset_final$Age[dataset_final$Z == "DZ"]), "\n") #Median
```

#### 2.3.4 BMI checks

```{r}
cat("Mean BMI: ", mean(dataset_final$BMI), "\n") #Mean Age
cat("SD BMI:   ", sd(dataset_final$BMI), "\n")
cat("Median:   ", median(dataset_final$BMI), "\n") #Median
cat("Lowest: ", min(dataset_final$BMI), "\n") #Youngest
cat("Highest:   ", max(dataset_final$BMI), "\n") #Oldest

print("MZ")
cat("Mean BMI: ", mean(dataset_final$BMI[dataset_final$Z == "MZ"]), "\n") #Mean Age
cat("SD BMI:   ", sd(dataset_final$BMI[dataset_final$Z == "MZ"]), "\n")
cat("Median:   ", median(dataset_final$BMI[dataset_final$Z == "MZ"]), "\n") #Median

cat("Lowest: ", min(dataset_final$BMI[dataset_final$Z == "MZ"]), "\n") #Youngest
cat("Highest:   ", max(dataset_final$BMI[dataset_final$Z == "MZ"]), "\n") #Oldest

print("DZ")
cat("Mean BMI: ", mean(dataset_final$BMI[dataset_final$Z == "DZ"]), "\n") #Mean Age
cat("SD BMI:   ", sd(dataset_final$BMI[dataset_final$Z == "DZ"]), "\n")
cat("Lowest: ", min(dataset_final$BMI[dataset_final$Z == "DZ"]), "\n") #Youngest
cat("Highest:   ", max(dataset_final$BMI[dataset_final$Z == "DZ"]), "\n") #Oldest
cat("Median:   ", median(dataset_final$BMI[dataset_final$Z == "DZ"]), "\n") #Median
```

#### BMI Graph

```{r}
#Graph
ggplot(BMI_data, 
       aes(x = BMI, fill = Group, color = Group)) + #Fill
        geom_density(alpha = 0.2)+ #Line thickness
        geom_density(aes(color = Group), alpha = 0, linewidth = 0.8) + #Adding mean lines
        geom_vline(data = plot_data %>%
        group_by(Group) %>%
        summarize(m = mean(BMI)),
        aes(xintercept = m, color = Group),
        linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 0.8) +

#If want side by side:
#facet_wrap(~Group) +

        #Aesthetics
        labs(title = "BMI Distributions", x = "BMI (kg/m²)",y = "Density") +
        scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "viridis") +
        scale_color_viridis_d(option = "viridis") +
        theme_minimal() +
        theme(legend.position = "right",
              plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
              panel.grid.major = element_blank(), 
              panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
              axis.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
              axis.text = element_text(size = 10)
        )

#Saving plot
ggsave(
  filename = "bmi_dis.png", 
  path = output_path,
  width = 10,     
  height = 5
)
```

Note: Check if twins got tested on different plates/times.

What about the season covariate? is it evenly distributed? if there were more twins measured in winter/summer how might allergies or seasonal immune boost affect it?

## 3. 'Olink_associations.RData' Exploration

This file contains the Olink association results and contains the objects: acc_list, infoF, olink_ass, olink_modules and tr.

### 3.1 Loading the data

```{r}
load("~/Twins_Project/data/raw_olink_ip/results/Olink_associations.RData")
```

The data frame tr is the focus of the project as it contains the FDR-significant results including the beta, se, p-values corresponding with the summary association statistics.

### 3.2 Structure Checks

Exploring how the data is organised and what data types it features.

```{r}
head(tr) 
dim(tr)
```

Each row represents the relationship between a specific immune phenotype (IP) and protein.

### 3.3 Protein Investigations

```{r}
#Unique proteins
length(unique(tr$Protein)) 

#Protein frequency
tr %>%
  count(Protein, sort = TRUE)

tr %>%
  count(Protein, sort = TRUE)%>% 
  tail(10)

```

There are 65 unique proteins. Meaning 65 proteins from original 74 that are correlated with immune cells from the flow cytometry.

### 3.4 Lineage Investigations

```{r}
#Unique lineages
length(unique(tr$Lineage))

#Lineages of cells
tr %>%
  count(Lineage, sort = TRUE)

#Not sure why but last page isnt showing
tr %>%
  count(Lineage, sort = TRUE)%>% 
  tail(10)

```

There are 27 unique lineages.

```{r}
sessionInfo()

```

------------------------------------------------------------------------
